speaker: interviewee
text: in the camp, besides starting the wurst factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1946, October of 1946, I met my wife. Of course, before that, before my future wife, I met a girl in the camp there, and we used to go around. And in October 1946, we were married, happily married. We are still married now, and it's been 50 years. But going back to that period, that was 1946, October. And I mentioned that we opened a [? wurstfabrik, ?] my brother and I, and we worked. When my wife was pregnant with our daughter-- pregnant women have an urge for certain things-- pickles. She had an urge for frankfurters. And frankfurters was a tough proposition to do by hand, because we had a factory made, but it was like a Rube Goldberg factory. It wasn't a regular-- with all the machinery. We had some machines. And some things we had to do by hand. My brother heard, in the evening, Rose wants frankfurters. He went down-- it wasn't-- there was-- the work was already done for the day. He went down, rolled up his sleeves, took out-- we had ground meat there, started working on the meat. Got himself some thin casings, filled up frankfurters, cooked them, smoked them. And he brought, the same day, up frankfurters to satisfy what she felt, you know, she needed. That was some moment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was-- your daughter was born in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the oldest daughter was born in July the 9th, 1948, in Germany. And a few months after that-- and in the meantime, yes, we registered ourselves to go to the United States. And my brother-- remember, I knew we had relatives in the United States, but I was too young to remember names of any aunts or uncles. But this brother, my half brother, was older than me, at least eight, nine years. And he remembered names. And he wrote to the-- he remembered names, but no addresses. And we used to get-- he used to read the [? Forwit ?] in Germany-- used to supply the UNRRA. It was an organization. The Americans used to apply to [? Forwit. ?] They used to read Yiddish paper, fluent. And he wrote the letter to [? Forwit, ?] to the [? Forwit ?] people, that he remembers our father. In other words, our uncles and aunts left after the first World War to the United States. These are the names. We know they settled in New York somewhere, and in Paterson, New Jersey. He remembered, but no addresses, just city. He wishes they should help find these relatives. These are the only relatives we have left now. And obviously, from the names, the [? Forwit ?] found our relatives. And they wrote a letter, and he wrote them back. And we had already contact with the United States. And they started working here, in the United States, to send papers for him first, then for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your wife also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- of course, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was in the camp when you met her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in different camps, entirely different camps than I was. She was in women camps, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you met, she was in the same place where you, when you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Salzheim. In Salzheim--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Salzheim.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] She was there, in fact, she was there with two other sisters, had two older sisters got married in Bergen-Belsen. After the liberation in 1945, they got married. And they also came from by Bergen-Belsen to Salzheim, to settle in Salzheim near Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was able to-- when you were married, you could both come without any problems or--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't easy. We had to register ourselves in the American Consulate that we want to go. So we worked from two fronts. Our family tried to make us papers from the United States. And the Americans wanted to liquidate the camps-- '48, say enough, let's give back the Germans the homes, and we'll take you to the United States. So we figured whichever comes first, we know we'll be candidates for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a couple or as individuals?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a couple, because we already, were already married, and we had, already, a daughter. Our daughter that was born July the 9th, 1948. And they-- they announced, in 1948, that we're going to liquidate the camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had a baby girl, and we get-- so they took us to a camp that was so-called a temporary camp, till they call us to emigrate to the United States. So we went, I remember, the new camp, Kassel, near the city of Kassel. There was a city-- the city of Kassel was bombarded a lot by the Allies, because they had their factories that made parts for airplanes. And they knew, the Americans, that without these parts, the plane cannot fly. And they bombarded these factories, they leveled them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they created a camp near Kassel, a temporary camp till they called us to the United States. And there came at time in February, middle of February, that they called us, the office called us. They said, no more Kassel, now you're going to go to the United States. We'll take you to a camp where we going to x-ray you and get you ready for the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1949. Ma-- February of 1949, because I said we had to leave in 1948, Salzheim, and they took us to this temporary camp at Kassel. So when they call us, when our papers get ready to be processed, they'll call us. And sure enough, our papers were processed, and they called us. And there was another camp, Butzbach, where they took you into that camp-- means you're waiting only for the ship to arrive. And when the ships arrive, they take you to the port city, and you travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time, we were with the little girl-- four, she was born in July-- that was, yeah, February-- well, six, seven months, eight months, and they called us to come to the United States. So we went through all the tests, all the questions, all the answers. But they obviously worked with our relatives. Our papers were not strictly from the American government.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in conjunction with the uncles, because our uncle had to guarantee-- at that time, they had to guarantee, before they let you in, that you will not fall, and the United States government will not have to support you-- welfare, whatever. And my uncle had to guarantee that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had a friend who was an insurance man, who was also a notary. He notarized the papers. They went into a butcher. He said my nephew is coming, he needs a job. But they told him, he wouldn't bother you, because he knows what to do and he'll find-- sign us, so we can come. So this butcher, I remember, in Washington Heights signed for me. And my uncle's friend, an insurance man and also a public notary, signed the papers, legit. They sent me that the job is ready. So in conjunction with them, between the two-- the United States government and my uncle-- the papers were fixed, and they took us. And we went and left for the United States in March of 1945, ah, '49. I'm sorry. And you came here, and where did you live, and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, when we came here, we arrived in Boston Harbor, because in New   York, was on strike, so we arrived in Boston. Boston, they put us on a train, because our relatives, who vouched for us-- an uncle of mine from Paterson, New Jersey, said you can come and live with me for a while till you get on your own feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came. We came by train to New York, Penn Station. And obviously, the UNRRA told them when the train, excuse me, is going to arrive. We arrived at the train station. They were waiting for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us, the family-- my wife, myself, and my daughter-- to Paterson, New Jersey, to a beautiful home-- Uncle [? Fein. ?] And we stood there two weeks, by this uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the American Jewish Organization-- it was a branch off the UJA, of the HIAS of the [? Joint]-- ?] one of those that knew that I arrived, and I'm staying with an uncle and aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they send me in a check, money, to buy food, clothes for the child. I opened this up, and I started to shake. I said to my wife, I can't accept charity. I'm a young man. I took this check, I told my aunts, where is this address. I want to go there in person. I want to go there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they told me, it's Market Street, they had an office there. I went over, and I said, look, I appreciate very much what you're trying to do. He said, we give you this for months to get-- I said, my brother came here two weeks before me.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, I mentioned before, that first they sent papers, my brother left two or four weeks before me, and he already had a job working in the union as a butcher. And I asked him, Avrum, how do you do-- He said where the union is. So I said to them, thank you so much. I appreciate what you want to do, but I can't accept it. I don't want no charity. This is a free country. I see, you can get a job. Everything's available only if you want to work. I want to work. I got to find a job for myself. So they say, we have no choice if you don't want it, we can't help it. In the meantime, this aunt went for a vacation, another aunt took us in. With this first aunt, we were two weeks, the other aunt, we're maybe a month or six weeks. But since I decided I wanted a job, I went-- me as a greenhorn, just came off the boat-- from Paterson, New Jersey, traveled by bus and by train, subway, to Union Square, walked over to the union-- couldn't speak English, a few words, here and there. And I went by train. I found everything. Went to the union. I told them, I don't know how they make baloney, here, but I'm a baloney maker from Europe. It may not be right for you, but I want a job as a baloney maker.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, where do you live? I said in Paterson, New Jersey. He said, you have to travel 2 hours to the job, work 8 to 10 hours and travel 2-- no, sorry. We can't give you a job. If you would live in New York, would consider it, why not, give you a crack at it. I come home. I said to  Rose, Rose, we got to find an apartment in New York. We got to move out to my aunt. Over there is plenty work available. I say the union has jobs. They read, every day, 4 o'clock jobs available. I can get a job. What do we have to sit? We have food. We have everything. We can buy everything we want. So we left the baby with the aunt. We took a ride to New York, my wife and I. I took-- I already had the experience. I traveled on it twice of three times from Paterson. I said, don't worry, we won't get lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took her in, she looks at the houses. She was surprised. But anyway, we came to New York, went to the East Side. A lot of Jewish people were living-- oh, by the way, yes, in Paterson, New Jersey-- in Paterson, where I was-- there were people from Belgium, also refugees that came to Paterson. This lady told my wife-- my wife was with my daughter in a carriage in the park-- and she was sitting in a park on a bench and started talking. She says you know, I have a sister in New York. Her name is Judith Greenberg, and she's got a apartment on the East Side.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wants to move to Brooklyn. Do you know anybody who would like an apartment? And my wife said, I would love an apartment in New York. She said good. I'll give you the address. That's how we came to this apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife took a ride to New York. In fact, we had two or three people, addresses that want to sell. One, I recently met, that lives at Century Village, Boca, [? Mung. ?] We went to his apartment first on the Lower East Side, an apartment, no windows, toilet in the hall. If you call it an apartment, it's a crime, yet people lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my wife, look I gotta get out from Paterson, because it's a small town. I can't get a job here. I got-- I'll take this. She said this without windows? She'll choke. The baby will choke, here. She started crying. I said, OK, OK. Let's go look at the other apartment. We went to the other one. It was a five-floor walk-up, but at least it had windows, the sun came in. And she said, would you-- I said would you settle for this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said this is already a mansion compared to the other one, no windows. And besides, we had already an indoor toilet, a tub in the kitchen-- a bathtub in the kitchen covered with a plate, like, as a table next to the sink.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she said this is already-- dear, fine, this is beautiful compared to the other one. I said, would you settle for that? She said, yeah. And I go out to the lady, this Judy Greenberg-- her husband was at work-- what will it take? She says, $300. To rent, per month?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to buy.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, to buy, to buy the apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a little furniture, there. $300, it was a fortune at that time. It was a fortune. I had no choice. I said OK. I bought the apartment for $300. The walls were crooked. They had mountains sticking out. I paper hanged. Whatever I could, I put wallpaper on. I made it into a bonbonniere. It looked so nice like a box of candy, of chocolate-- that the superintendent, who we paid the rent-- whenever she wanted to rent an apartment, she says, come, I gotta show you an apartment. She brought everybody to us, because I papered the whole apartment. It looked-- the, the, the hunchbacks on the wall were straight covered. It was nice. Everybody should she took in here to our apartment to show it. And the rent was a big $16 a month. 1-6, $16. I had the apartment. I went to the union. I got a job as a baloney maker, but they didn't like the way I was doing it. So after two hours, the man fired me. But-- and he told me, look, I have nothing against you, except I need the production, and you are not right. Tell the union you want a job as a helper first. You know what to do, but you haven't got the experience, he said-- how we do it here. Take a job as a helper. You, I'm sure, as a helper, you can learn while you have time, and you will be OK. I listened to the man. I was crying. I was so happy. I came to the United States, and here, everything is available. You get a job and live and work. The man-- I took the man's advice. I went to the union, I said look. You sent me as a baloney maker. The guy didn't like we. I worked only two hours, and he sent home. He advised me, I should take a job as a helper, a butcher's helper.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you get-- He said, sure, we have plenty of work for helpers. I said, good. I got that. I got my job. I earned money. They gave me-- I was very willing to work. They gave me plenty of overtime to work. And thank God, from that job, I learned to be a butcher, in other words, a hard cutter, that was a place with [? four. ?] I learned to do everything-- trim loins, trim hams, cut the spare ribs. I learned everything. I was capable, and I was young, what should I tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the helper's pay was, at that time, $1.25 an hour. Here, as a butcher, I already got $1.68 an hour. That was a lot of money, the difference, here. And besides, the boss liked me, because I was a good worker. He gave me overtime, and we made a very good living. We were very happy. Then came a time we accumulated a few dollars, and I was offered a business. So we went into luncheonette business, but I lost my money there. It didn't work out. Went back to work. So I didn't go anymore to this. I went to a line that was closer to what we had in Europe-- corned beef, pastrami, tongues. I went to such a place. I learned this trade. Then came another few years later, another opportunity, a few guys who we knew the trade. They said, let's go. We can work for somebody. Lets go business for ourselves. And we went into business for ourselves, five people. We opened up a place. We were struggling at the beginning, but it worked out, and knock on wood, we are fine. And I was in this business till I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon? Yes, yes, well, I missed-- in 1952, my other daughter-- I'm sorry, I missed it, because it happens, you can't remember everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our daughter, second daughter, was born on January 30, 1952, in downtown New York, where we lived in that place. She was born in Bellevue Hospital. And I think she was the only quite blonde baby in the world, there. Because they came in, that's all. But that was our other daughter, Sandy. And what was the name of your business that you-- your business, what was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: First, it was Brook Boneless. And then came in the Wholesome Meat Act, a new law the Department of Agriculture passed that every place, it's got to be federal inspected. We were, in the first business we bought, we were state inspected, New York State. So we couldn't ship meats interstate. We had only to sell the state of New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came out, the Wholesome Meat Act in-- that was either 1969 or 1968, and they said everybody has to convert federal inspection, meaning you will be able to sell all over the country, to every state, even out of the country, to foreign countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: We merged our firm, this Brook Boneless, merged with two other firms. There was another firm, Ace Beef, and one, Ajax Packing. We used to do business before amongst each other. However, a father of these people that we ordered, he used to come to our place and saw how we, the newcomers, work. He said-- and these were American people-- he said Bernie, the father said to one, join with these guys. They're good workers. They work hard. They're honest. Merge with them. You'll be successful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the offer came from them, because this is a question of time-- either we'll have to out of business, or merge and create a larger place. And they came to us with the offer, and we said, did you look out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, yeah, we saw a place that we could buy, and we could create one company. And we bought that place, and we merged with these people. We created a firm that was called Consolidated Beef, in Jamaica, New York. And we were very successful. We opened that up in 1970. And I was there till I retired. In 1985, and we sold it. I'm very happy I'm a retired person, now, for the last 11 years, and I'm very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to talk more at the end of the next tape and see how things have happened since, how your family has grown.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something we haven't covered that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- yes, I remember two incidents in the ghetto that we skipped. Since I mentioned before, I was hungry for four years in the ghetto. People organized a protest march in the ghetto. And a lot of people joined in, because the protest was-- give us some additional food. This is not enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we marched with placards, like here, demonstrations, peaceful demonstration. We weren't out to hurt anybody, just marching and stating what our demands are. The demonstration went on for a half an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: German trucks came in, mounted with machine guns, and just started shooting at the demonstrators. That's an incident I forgot. I just remembered it, and I figured it should be on record, because that's what happened. That was the end the demonstration. People ran into the houses and the gates. They left. That was the only demonstration in the Lodz ghetto. That was the end. However, in the interim, in the ghetto, the Germans always asked Mr. Rumkowski for people-- 10,000 people, 15,000 people. And these people were never heard of. These were the people that supplied the clothes-- what I mentioned, we select a shirt, shirt-- that was. There came a time in 1942 where they needed, not 10,000, 15,000. They wanted 50,000, 60,000. And he said, I can't give you that. My resorts will be empty. I wouldn't have workers, who, to produce stuff for you. They said, if you wouldn't, we'll come in and take it ourselves. And they did. They called it [? the schpera, ?] like, you are to stay home, so you cannot go out in the street. And they came in, the Germans, in the ghetto, and the Jewish Police worked with them. They encircled a square block of houses, and said, everybody out, whoever is going to remain home is going to be shot on the spot, so better everybody go down to the street. We went down to the street. We're gathered in a big place in the street, And they had set up lines that narrowed. The crowd was standing. We had to walk till we narrowed like cattle, came, like, individually, we had to go through. And there was a guy, maybe it was [? Mengele ?] from Auschwitz, I don't know. I don't know the man, so I can't say. It was one guy who looked at the face, and was selection-- one to this side, one to this side, just like your heard the selections from Auschwitz. That was quite a few times, and they took out many people from the ghetto, and they were never heard of. Their clothes-- because people recognized some of the clothes that came back-- was from their neighbors. These are two incidents I wanted to mention.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was taken out before, my sister. Again, she went, what we now know was near Lodz. It was-- they used to gas the people in the truck, while we're still traveling, and dumping them in ditches. We know that. My sister--in fact, my sister said to my brother, Abraham, don't worry. I got two kilograms of gold in my girdle, hidden. And I'll survive the war anywhere. Little did they know that they'll have to undress and leave everything. From the experiences I had later in Buchenwald and in Dora, I know what happened to her gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And we'll go on at the end of the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
